error.signature: AI violates Japanese gift-giving customs (お中元, お歳暮, wrapping rituals)
error.regex: (Japanese.*gift|お中元|お歳暮|内祝い|祝儀|不祝儀|Japanese.*wrapping|furoshiki)

verdict.summary: Japanese gift-giving has elaborate rules about quantity (avoid 4 and 9), wrapping direction, seasonal timing, and reciprocity. The presentation is often more important than the gift itself.

dead_ends.action: Give gifts in sets of 4 or 9
dead_ends.why_fails: 4 (四/shi) sounds like death (死/shi) and 9 (九/ku) sounds like suffering (苦/ku). Tea sets of 4, gift boxes of 9 items — these numbers are avoided in all Japanese gift contexts.

dead_ends.action: Open a gift immediately in front of the giver
dead_ends.why_fails: In Japan, gifts are not opened in front of the giver (unlike Western custom). Opening immediately can appear greedy. Accept with both hands, express gratitude, and open later in private.

dead_ends.action: Wrap gifts casually or use dark-colored wrapping
dead_ends.why_fails: Japanese gift wrapping (包装) is an art. Wrapping direction matters: right over left for celebrations (慶事), left over right for funerals (弔事). Getting this backwards invokes death.

workarounds.action: Give in sets of 3, 5, or 7 (lucky odd numbers in Japan). Wrap in light, seasonal colors. Use noshi (熨斗) paper for formal gifts with appropriate mizuhiki (水引) knot style.
Observe seasonal gift cycles: お中元 (mid-year, July) and お歳暮 (year-end, December) for business/social obligations. Budget 3,000-5,000 yen for colleagues, 5,000-10,000 for superiors.
When giving, say 'つまらないものですが' (it's nothing special) — modesty is expected even for expensive gifts. Present and receive with both hands. Reciprocate gifts of similar value (内祝い).

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Both cultures avoid 4 due to death homophone but Japanese also avoids 9 (suffering) and has elaborate wrapping direction rules absent in Chinese culture
